title: COM Call-In to Uniface

COM Call-In to Uniface

COM Call-In to Uniface

To a COM client such as Visual Basic, a Uniface service that is exposed as a COM class appears to be a normal COM class. The calling client does not need to know anything specific about Uniface for the conversation. All Uniface details are hidden from the caller.

Uniface uses a configuration definition to represent the environment in which the Uniface services with a COM implementation run. This is a combination of assignment file, initialization file, application shell, command line options, working directory, and /bin directory. These names are kept under the Uniface area of the Registry, maintained by the Uniface COM Configuration tool. Each exported COM Interface DLL has an assigned, embedded name.

The COM connector acts as an in-process COM server. It is a dynamically loaded library (DLL) that uses the Uniface 3GL call-in facility to create a Uniface environment and run services that appear as COM objects. It is not important how services are started, for example, a normal Uniface executable as opposed to a call-in environment. However, the standard Uniface GUI may not be available, depending on how the service is being executed.

To determine the Uniface configuration to start, the connector reads a configuration definition name that is embedded in the COM Interface DLL and retrieves the configuration values for the session.

Note: It is not possible for a Uniface component to activate another Uniface component via a COM interface when they are running in the same Uniface process. This is only possible if they are running in different processes.

To run the COM connector as a local (separate process) server, it can be loaded by the standard Microsoft Windows DLL surrogate. You can use the OLE-COM Object Viewer (available from Microsoft, for example in Visual C++) to enable this behavior. With the local-process server, there is no limitation on the number of different Uniface configurations that a single COM client can access simultaneously through COM-exposed services. Each configuration runs in its own process space.

When using the facilities of Microsoft Windows COM, there is no notable difference to the client, whether the Uniface service is running locally or remotely. The difference is in the Microsoft Windows Registry and is not a Uniface configuration issue. The standard Microsoft Windows DCOMCNFG program can expose a local server as a remote server.

The type library must be installed on the client workstation and the client’s Registry settings must point to the server workstation.

Remote COM activation requires the correct COM security settings. Consult your Microsoft documentation and the Microsoft web site for more information.

Related Links
  • Enabling COM Call-In

    For a COM client to use Uniface services it needs to know the components’ interface definitions. These are contained in a standard Microsoft Windows type library, which is contained in a standard Microsoft Windows self-registering DLL. The type library DLL must be installed on the client workstation and the client’s Registry settings must point to the server workstation.

  • Configuring Uniface for COM Support

    The Uniface COM connector allows ProcScript to call out to COM objects, and COM clients to call in to Uniface components, without either side of the conversation knowing the implementation details.
